title: Quick check: creating a new organisation

questions.prompt: Why is getting the organisation name right the first time so important?
choices: Renames after the fact are messy and often leave stale references in incident notification templates and PSA mappings.

choices.label: Huntress charges a fee for organisation renames.
choices.rationale: Renames are free but operationally expensive in downstream impact.

choices.label: The name field doesn't accept changes after the first save.
choices.rationale: Renames are technically possible.

choices.label: Renames break the agent's enrolment token.
choices.rationale: Agent enrolment uses an org key, not the display name.

questions.explanation: Renames leave stale references in incident notifications and PSA mappings. Get the name right at creation.

questions.prompt: You just saved a new organisation but the Agents tab shows 4 endpoints already enrolled. What does this mean?
choices: Stop. You may be looking at the wrong org, the wrong portal, or someone else has started the onboarding in parallel. Bump.

choices.label: The agents are pre-enrolled; this is fine, proceed.
choices.rationale: A brand-new org should never have pre-existing agents. Pre-existing enrolments are a duplicate-onboarding flag.

choices.label: The numbers will reset within an hour; check back.
choices.rationale: The portal doesn't reset enrolment counts; the agents are really there.

choices.label: Contact Huntress support; the portal is showing test data.
choices.rationale: Misuses support. The issue is internal, a stale, wrong-tenant, or in-progress onboarding by someone else.

questions.explanation: Pre-existing state on a brand-new org is a stop signal. The blank-state check exists exactly for this.

questions.prompt: "Custom one-off notification routing per customer" is named as a common mistake. Why?
choices: The MSP's partner-level notification template keeps on-call rotations, PSA integration, and incident routing consistent. One-off routing decays the moment the original tech leaves.

choices.label: Custom routing breaks Huntress's audit log.
choices.rationale: Not a real platform behaviour.

choices.label: Huntress charges extra for custom notification routing.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the commercial model.

choices.label: Custom routing is forbidden by the schema and will throw an error.
choices.rationale: It's technically allowed; it's just operationally bad.

questions.explanation: One-off routing decays operationally; the partner-level template keeps the platform consistent across techs and customers.

questions.prompt: You're creating an org for "Northwind Logistics Pty Ltd" per the runbook. You spot an existing org called "Northwind" with 8 agents and 3 closed incidents from last year. What do you do?

choices.label: Create the new org and ignore the existing one; the names differ.
choices.rationale: Premature. The existing org could be the same customer mid-rename, a different customer with a similar name, or a previous incomplete onboarding. Don't create in parallel without checking.

choices: Stop, bump to a senior: "Existing org 'Northwind' has 8 agents, same customer, different one, or incomplete onboarding? Should I reuse, rename, or create new?"

choices.label: Rename the existing 'Northwind' to 'Northwind Logistics - Tier 2' and reuse it.
choices.rationale: Worst response. You've renamed an org with agents and incidents on an assumption. If it isn't the same customer, you've broken another customer's portal.

questions.explanation: The pre-existing org is the signal you need a senior call before clicking anything.

questions.prompt: The senior replies: "Different customer. The old 'Northwind' is a stale prospect we never billed. Create the new org, and bump the stale one to me, I'll deactivate it." What do you do?
choices: Create the new org. Leave the stale one alone; the senior said they'd handle it.

choices.label: Create the new org, then deactivate the stale 'Northwind' since the senior confirmed it's stale.
choices.rationale: "I'll deactivate it" was the senior taking the action; they didn't delegate. Permanent deactivation always escalates regardless.

choices.label: Create the new org and rename the stale one to 'DELETE_ME_OLD_NORTHWIND' so it's obvious.
choices.rationale: The senior is going to deactivate it anyway. 'DELETE_ME' prefixes linger and confuse search for a year.

questions.explanation: The senior is explicit about owning the stale-org cleanup. You do the part you own (create), document the bump in your notes, move on.

questions.prompt: Which of these org-creation situations should trigger an escalation BEFORE saving?
choices: The customer asks for branding or notification customisation that diverges from the partner template.

choices.label: The runbook fully specifies all values needed and you've confirmed them.
choices.rationale: Standard tech work, proceed.

choices.label: The customer's tier is one your MSP has worked with hundreds of times.
choices.rationale: Standard tech work, apply the documented template.

choices.label: The branding template renders correctly in the customer-view portal.
choices.rationale: Success path of verification, not an escalation trigger.

questions.explanation: One-off customisations are senior-owned. Bump as a product-mix-style question.

questions.prompt: Why does the lesson explicitly NOT embed the MSP's onboarding runbook directly?
choices: Runbooks drift fast and the MSP's version is the source of truth.

choices.label: Huntress's documentation team writes the runbook, not the MSP.
choices.rationale: The MSP owns and maintains the runbook.

choices.label: Embedding the runbook would breach Huntress's licence terms.
choices.rationale: Not the lesson's reason.

choices.label: Runbooks are confidential and can't appear in training material.
choices.rationale: The framing is about avoiding stale embedded copies, not confidentiality.

questions.explanation: The MSP's runbook is the source of truth; the lesson teaches the shape, not the values.

questions.prompt: "The form saved, must be fine, skip the verify step." What's the risk?
choices: You miss the case where you started against the wrong runbook or another tech has already done part of the onboarding. Two minutes of verification, zero minutes of regret.

choices.label: Huntress retains the unsaved data on the server; the form might not have actually saved.
choices.rationale: The verify step isn't about save reliability, it's about state correctness.

choices.label: Skipping verify breaks the audit log.
choices.rationale: Not the lesson's reason.

choices.label: There is no risk, modern portals are reliable enough that verify is paranoia.
choices.rationale: Inverts the lesson's named common-mistake category.

questions.explanation: Two minutes of verification catches wrong-runbook or duplicate-onboarding situations the form-saved check can't see.

questions.prompt: Put the org-creation steps in the right order.
items: Open the runbook and confirm naming convention, branding template, notification routing, and tier label.
Open the Organisations list and click Add Organisation.
Set the organisation name per the runbook convention.
Apply the branding template and set notification routing per the partner-level template.
Tag the tier label and save.
Verify the blank state (0 agents, no integrations, no incidents) before moving on.
questions.explanation: Read the runbook before clicking; do the portal work in the documented order; verify the blank state catches wrong-tenant or duplicate-onboarding signals.

questions.prompt: A brand-new organisation in Huntress that shows pre-existing agents, integrations, or incident history is normal and can be proceeded with.
questions.explanation: A new org should be blank. Pre-existing state means wrong tenant, wrong portal, or someone else started the onboarding in parallel. Stop and bump.
